The Planning Bookshelf · free

The Planning Bookshelf

A curated digital library for UK planning professionals — the definitive and the essential, from planning law to placemaking. Tap any cover to buy from the publisher or retailer; purchases happen off the app.

Spine one

The Application Journey

Six stages from first advice to final outcome.

Sets your default level across every stage. Practitioner Notes are a Premium feature.

The Development Plan Journey

Plans are where planning decisions are really made — years before any application. Plan-making is in transition under the P&I Act 2025; transitional arrangements determine which regime applies.

How To

Do-this-then-that guides to the tasks planners, agents and applicants actually carry out. Free.

Free · events & CPD

What’s On

Upcoming planning events, conferences, training and CPD across England — from the RTPI and its regions, the TCPA, POS/PAS and the main policy forums. Listings are gathered automatically and refreshed weekly; always confirm the date, price and availability on the organiser’s own page before booking or travelling.

Technology & climate · free and premium

Technology & Climate

How technology and the changing climate are reshaping planning, development and the built environment — and, honestly, where each is heading. The technology articles are free; the climate article is premium. Throughout, the aim is an honest picture that separates what is real from what is being sold.

Technology & Innovation
How to read this section. Every article is tagged by maturity — In use (working today), Emerging (being trialled), or On the horizon (plausible, in development) — and by planning impact. No products are named or endorsed; the aim is an honest picture, not a sales pitch.
Climate & Environment

England / UK · each article dated and tagged for maturity and impact. Written for planners, not for a tech audience.

Free · Technology · anchor article

The Future of Planning

How digital technology is reshaping practice — and what the next five to ten years could realistically bring. Status: spans In use → On the horizon. Impact: High. England; written as at 19 July 2026 — the fastest-moving area in the profession, so re-check any dated statement before relying on it.

Free · Technology · article 2

Artificial Intelligence in Planning

What it does for the job today, where it is heading, and how to use it without getting burned. Status: In use → Emerging. Impact: High. England/UK; written as at 19 July 2026. Two hard truths this article does not soften: AI hallucinates confidently, and the accountability never leaves the planner.

Free · Technology · article 3

Planning Data & Spatial Intelligence

From reading documents to combining datasets — and the friction that stops it being simple. Status: In use → On the horizon. Impact: High. England/UK; written as at 19 July 2026. Two hard truths: much of the most useful data is fragmented or licensed, and “the data exists” is not “I can trust it, and lawfully use it, for this purpose”.

What’s new

Updates & Improvements

What has been added or improved in PlanRef, and when. The app is actively maintained — new tools, guides and corrections are logged here as they ship.

Separately, the Recent Changes feed on the home screen is checked daily against official sources (legislation.gov.uk, GOV.UK, the Planning Inspectorate and the courts) and refreshed each morning.

Free · How To · guide 1

How to Submit a Planning Application

A practical, do-this-then-that walkthrough: what a valid application contains, where to upload it, the step-by-step on the portal, and how to make sure it is registered rather than rejected. The first guide in PlanRef’s How To series. England; current to July 2026 — portal names, service charges and validation lists change, so always check your target authority’s current local validation list.

Free · How To · guide 2

How to Become a Chartered Town Planner

Every route to MRTPI — from an accredited planning degree, from another discipline, from planning admin, from surveying, or by apprenticeship — and the APC that ends them all. UK; current to July 2026. The RTPI reviews its routes, word counts, experience requirements and fees periodically, so confirm the current detail at rtpi.org.uk before you commit time or money.

Free · How To · guide 3

How to Write a Delegated Officer Report

The document a case officer produces more than any other, and the one that decides most applications — around nine in ten are determined under delegated powers, and for those the report is the decision. How to structure it, how to write each part, and how to keep it defensible when a disappointed objector or applicant reads it looking for a way in. England; current to July 2026.

Free · Foundations essay

First Principles

What development is, why permitted development is still planning permission, and what it takes to begin. A foundations essay for anyone working in English planning — free, in full. Twelve short parts building one discipline: is it development; where does permission come from; and has it lawfully begun? England only; general guidance, not advice on a specific proposal.

Salaries & progression · premium

Salaries & Progression

What planners earn across the UK — by grade, sector, region, chartership and specialism. A predictable, source-backed explorer plus the core reference tables. Indicative market ranges, not a promise: a compass, not a contract.

Careers · premium · flagship guide

The Reputation That Gets You Hired

A recruiter’s guide to building a profile, CV and presence that makes employers want to recruit you — for planners at every stage. United Kingdom · 2026.

Most people think recruitment begins the moment they send a CV. After more than thirty years placing planners — from graduates to directors — the truth is it begins long before that, and it never really stops. By the time your application lands, your name has been searched, your LinkedIn read, and any appeal decisions, talks or online comments seen too. The CV is not the start of the process; it is the moment a picture that already exists gets confirmed or contradicted.

That is not meant to alarm you — it is meant to reframe how you think about your career. You are not a document assessed a few times a decade when you happen to be job-hunting. You are a reputation, built continuously, that exists online whether you tend it or not, and that employers and recruiters consult before they ever speak to you. The planners who get the best jobs, and the fastest promotions, are rarely the ones with the cleverest CV tricks. They are the ones whose reputation does the work before they walk in the room.

The first impression is made before you speak

UK recruiters, and what they do online before contact (StandOut CV, 2025).

Inside the full guide. Twelve chapters from three decades on the hiring side of the desk — your professional brand, an outstanding CV (with strong-vs-weak wording and a do’s-and-don’ts), the cover letter, LinkedIn for 2026, what employers actually check, protecting your digital reputation, interview preparation, building a career, salary progression, the ranked fifty mistakes that damage careers, and a six-month checklist — with worked examples, ten tables and full sources.

Part 1 — Building your professional brand

The word “brand” makes a lot of planners wince, because it sounds like marketing, and planners are, rightly, suspicious of marketing. So let me define it plainly. Your professional brand is simply the reputation that arrives before you do — what people say, think and expect about you when your name comes up and you are not in the room. It is not a logo or a slogan. It is the answer to the question a recruiter or employer quietly asks about every candidate: what is this person known for, and can I trust them?

Everyone already has one. The graduate who is always prepared has a brand. The senior planner known for winning difficult appeals has one. The officer whose reports are a pleasure to read has one — and so does the one whose reports are late and thin. A brand is not something you either have or lack; it is something you either shape or leave to chance. The only real choice is whether the reputation that precedes you is the one you would have chosen. Most people never think about it, which means most people’s brand is an accident. Yours does not have to be.

First impressions form fast, and increasingly online. In UK surveys of recruiters, around 91% use LinkedIn regularly and around 84% screen candidates on social platforms (StandOut CV, 2025). The first impression you make is usually not your handshake or even your CV; it is your LinkedIn photo, your headline, the tone of your last few posts, and whatever a search of your name returns. Those things are judged in seconds, and the judgement colours everything that follows.

Why trust is the whole game. Underneath a brand sits one thing: trust. Recruiters place, and employers hire and promote, people they trust to do the work, represent them well, and not become a problem. Everything in this guide — the honest CV, the consistent LinkedIn presence, the clean digital footprint, the reputation for helping colleagues — is really about building that trust and removing reasons to doubt it. A career is a long game of accumulated trust, and the planners who understand that early move faster and further.

Part 2 — Writing an outstanding CV

The CV still matters — it is where your reputation gets evidenced — so it is worth getting right. An excellent planning CV is not hard to write; it just takes discipline, honesty, and the avoidance of a few common mistakes.

Length, layout and the look of it. For almost everyone, two pages is the target; a graduate can manage on one or two, and only a genuinely senior planner with a long record needs a third. Keep the layout clean and conventional: a clear name and contact line, a short profile, then employment history in reverse order, education, qualifications and skills. Use a plain, readable font (Calibri, Arial, Georgia or similar) at a sensible size, generous but not wasteful margins, and consistent formatting. Use colour sparingly — a single restrained accent for headings is fine; a rainbow is not. Recruiters read hundreds of these, and clarity beats cleverness every time.

PDF, Word and the machines that read first. Send a PDF unless the employer or agency asks for Word, because a PDF preserves your formatting on any screen. The one caveat is the Applicant Tracking System (ATS) — the software many larger employers use to receive and search CVs. Exact UK prevalence is not reliably published, so treat that figure as uncertain, but the practical rule costs you nothing: make your CV machine-readable. That means a clean single-column layout, standard section headings (“Employment”, “Education”), no text inside images, graphics, text-boxes or headers/footers a parser may miss, and the real keywords a search would use — “chartered town planner”, “development management”, “MRTPI”, “Local Plan”, the software you use. A beautiful CV the machine cannot read never reaches the human.

The sections, and what goes in them. A planning CV should include: a short personal profile (three or four lines); employment history focused on achievements; education; professional qualifications and memberships — RTPI chartership (MRTPI) above all, plus RICS, ICE or others where relevant, and your CPD; and a skills section. For planners the skills worth naming specifically include GIS (say which — ArcGIS, QGIS), CAD where you use it, the Planning Portal and case-management systems, data analysis, and the substantive competencies employers search for: development management, planning policy, major applications, appeals, public inquiries, committee experience and presentation skills. Name the real things you have done; they are also the keywords a recruiter searches on.

The one rule that matters most: achievements, not duties. A weak CV lists duties — the things your job description says you were responsible for. A strong CV shows achievements — what you actually did, with a result and, wherever possible, a number. Every planner in a given role has the same duties; what separates you is what you achieved within them.

Weak — a dutyStrong — an achievement
Responsible for determining planning applications.Determined ~120 applications a year within statutory timescales, including major and contentious schemes.
Dealt with planning appeals.Prepared and presented evidence at six appeals, winning five, including a Green Belt housing hearing.
Good communication skills.Presented 30+ applications to committee, securing members’ support for the officer recommendation on complex cases.
Involved in the Local Plan.Led the housing chapter of the emerging Local Plan through Regulation 19 consultation and examination.
Used GIS software.Built constraints analyses in ArcGIS for a 200-site strategic land availability assessment.
Worked with developers and clients.Advised housebuilder clients from pre-application to consent on schemes totalling 800 homes, all approved.

Illustrative wording — use your own real figures; never inflate them.

Personal profile · before / after

Before: “A hardworking and motivated planner with excellent communication skills, looking for a new and exciting challenge where I can develop my career.”

After: “Chartered town planner (MRTPI) with seven years’ development-management experience across major residential and commercial schemes, a strong appeal record, and a track record of steering contentious applications through committee. Now seeking a principal role in a growth-focused consultancy.”

The first could be anyone; the second is a person a recruiter can place. Specificity is credibility.

CV do’s and don’ts

✓ Do✗ Don’t
Lead with achievements and resultsList duties copied from the job description
Keep to two pages; three only if seniorRun to four-plus pages of everything
Use clean, standard, ATS-readable formattingBury content in tables, text-boxes or columns
Tailor the emphasis to each roleSend the identical CV to every employer
Quantify wherever you honestly canInvent or inflate figures, dates or responsibilities
Proofread ruthlessly (and have someone else check)Leave typos — they read as carelessness
Name real software and competencies (keywords)Pad with vague adjectives — “dynamic”, “passionate”
Explain gaps briefly and honestlyHide or fudge dates — it looks worse when found

Part 3 — The cover letter

A cover letter, where one is asked for, should be one page — three or four short paragraphs — and it should do a different job from the CV. The CV is the evidence; the letter is the argument. It answers two questions the CV cannot: why this employer, and why you, for this role, now.

A simple structure works. Open with why them — something specific and genuine a template could not contain (a scheme they are working on, their reputation in a sector, their growth). Then why you — the two or three things about your experience that fit this role, with a concrete example, not a restatement of your CV. Then a short close: your enthusiasm, your availability, and a confident sign-off. Never copy your CV into prose; never send a generic letter with the company name swapped in (recruiters spot it instantly, and a wrong company name left in from the last application is a genuine, common, career-ending howler); and never make it about what you want from them rather than what you offer.

A cover letter in miniature

“I’ve followed [firm]’s work on the [named] regeneration scheme with real interest — strategic, contentious urban sites are exactly where I do my best work. Over the past four years I’ve taken three major residential applications from pre-application to consent, including one recovered at appeal, and I’m known for keeping difficult schemes moving and members onside. I’d relish bringing that to your growing development-management team, and I’m available at four weeks’ notice.”

Specific, evidenced, and about them as much as about you.

Part 4 — LinkedIn

For a modern professional, LinkedIn is not optional, because it is where recruiters and employers look first. Around 91% of recruiters use it regularly, and among specialist social recruiters effectively all of them use it to search for candidates (StandOut CV, 2025); the UK has some 30 million users. A strong profile is, in practice, a second CV that works while you sleep — one recruiters find proactively, bringing opportunities to you rather than the other way round.

Be findable. Recruiters find candidates by searching keywords, so your profile has to contain the words they search for. Think what an employer looking for you would type — “chartered town planner”, “development management”, “MRTPI”, “planning policy”, your city — and make sure those words appear naturally in your headline, about section and experience. The single most wasted piece of LinkedIn real estate is the headline, which most people leave as just their job title. Use it: “Chartered Town Planner (MRTPI) | Development Management & Major Applications | [City]” is human-readable and packed with the terms that make you findable. Your about section should be first person, tell a short professional story, carry your achievements and keywords, and read like you — not a CV pasted in.

Profile elementWhat good looks like
PhotoClear, friendly, professional head-and-shoulders. Approachable beats stiff; never a night-out crop.
BannerSimple and relevant — a cityscape, a scheme, a clean branded image. Not the default blank.
HeadlineRole + specialisms + location, not just a job title. Your prime keyword space.
AboutFirst person, short story, achievements, keywords. A person, not a CV.
ExperienceAchievements with results, mirroring the CV rule — not duty lists.
FeaturedPin your best work: an article, a talk, a project, a portfolio link.
Skills & endorsementsList the real ones recruiters filter on; endorsements add weight.
RecommendationsA few genuine ones from managers/clients — worth more than any self-description.
Open to WorkRecruiter-only setting if employed; the public green banner if openly searching.

The extras. Recommendations are the most under-used feature on LinkedIn and among the most persuasive — ask for them, specifically, after a job well done. Premium can help an active job-seeker with InMail and insights, and Creator mode suits those who post regularly, but neither is necessary for a strong profile.

Networking is being useful, not collecting contacts. Send connection requests with a short personal note rather than the blank default — it roughly doubles acceptance and starts a relationship. Follow the organisations and people who matter to your career: the RTPI, the local authorities and consultancies you admire, and the planning leaders whose thinking you respect. When you message a recruiter, be concise and specific — a vague “please find me a job” gets little; a targeted “I’m an MRTPI DM planner in the North West looking to move into consultancy, is that a market you work in?” gets a reply.

Posting, and the 2026 algorithm. You do not have to post to benefit, but a little thoughtful activity builds visibility and credibility over time. The algorithm changes and no one outside LinkedIn knows it exactly, so treat this as informed guidance, not gospel:

  • Consistency beats volume. Posting two to five times a week, steadily, does more than a burst then silence. A comment counts too.
  • Comments matter more than likes. The 2026 consensus is a comment is worth roughly twice a like to reach, because it signals real engagement.
  • Dwell time and early engagement count. Content people actually stop and read, and that gets engagement in the first hour, travels furthest.
  • Knowledge and stories perform; bait and links underperform. Genuine insight, a lesson from a case, a plain-English explainer or native video do well; engagement-bait and posts whose main content is an external link tend to be shown less.
Reach signal, roughly — a comment is worth about twice a like (2026 consensus).
Like ·1×
Comment ·2×

The aim is credibility, not celebrity. Two examples of the kind of post that works for a planner: a short honest reflection — “An appeal I lost last month taught me more than any win — here’s the point about heritage harm I’d underweighted, and how I’d argue it now” — or a plain-English explainer — “Biodiversity net gain in ninety seconds, for anyone who still finds it baffling.” Useful, human, and unmistakably from someone who knows the work.

Part 5 — Other social media

LinkedIn is the one platform that genuinely matters for a planning career. The rest range from mildly useful to actively risky, and the honest answer to “should I be on all of them?” is a firm no. Spread yourself across every platform and you will maintain none well and expose yourself on several.

PlatformWorth it?Why
LinkedInEssentialWhere recruiters and employers actually look. Non-negotiable.
Website / portfolioValuable for someStrong for consultants, urban designers and the self-employed; a clean shopfront.
Professional blogIf you enjoy itBuilds authority in a specialism; feeds LinkedIn. Optional.
X (Twitter)Optional, riskySome planning debate remains, but the tone is combative and the reputational risk real.
BlueSky / ThreadsEmergingSome conversation has moved here; low stakes, low return so far.
YouTubeNicheOnly if you genuinely make video content; otherwise ignore.
InstagramPersonalSome design-led practices use it; for most, a private space to lock down.
FacebookLock it downRarely a career asset; often a liability. Tighten privacy.
TikTokGenerally notA little young-planner content exists; for most, no career value and real risk.
RedditWith carer/urbanplanning is useful reading; posting is anonymous but not truly private.
GitHubIf relevantWorth it for planners doing data, scripting or GIS development; irrelevant for most.

The practical rule: invest in LinkedIn, keep a portfolio or blog if it suits your specialism, and treat everything else as personal space to be secured rather than a career tool. The other platforms far more often cost people opportunities than win them.

Part 6 — What employers check

Employers and recruiters do due diligence, and you should assume they do it on you — around 84% screen candidates on social media, and 21% have rejected a candidate because of what they found (StandOut CV, 2025). A typical check runs from the professional outward: your LinkedIn, then a plain Google of your name, then whatever public social media turns up — and, for planners specifically, sources most professions do not have. Planning Inspectorate decisions name the planners and advocates involved, so your appeal record is partly public. RTPI membership and professional disciplinary records can be checked. Companies House shows directorships. For senior hires, employers may look at published papers, conference talks, podcast appearances, articles and news coverage. Most of this, for a competent professional, is reassuring.

Creates confidenceCreates concern
A complete, consistent, professional LinkedInA blank or contradictory profile (dates that don’t match the CV)
Evidence of expertise: talks, articles, CPDNothing at all — no trace, no reference points
Thoughtful, civil engagement in the professionPublic arguments, pile-ons and combative threads
A clean, or sensibly private, personal footprintOffensive humour, slurs or discriminatory comments
RTPI membership and a clean recordDisciplinary findings or misrepresented qualifications
Genuine recommendations from managers/clientsAlcohol/drug imagery or party photos set to public
A coherent story across CV, letter and profileConspiracy theories, misinformation or extreme content

A word of fairness. Good employers, and CIPD guidance on selection, warn against over-reaching in social-media screening, because it can introduce bias and stray into personal life, and data-protection law applies. But you cannot control how carefully any given employer draws that line, so the safe assumption is that anything public may be seen. The point is not to become bland or fearful; it is to make sure what is public is professional or innocuous. Political views, held and expressed civilly, are your right; a stream of aggressive, offensive or extreme content is a different matter, and it does cost people jobs.

Part 7 — Your digital reputation

The single most important thing to understand about your online life is that nothing on it is ever completely private, and nothing is ever completely gone. A message can be screenshotted. A “private” group has members. A deleted post may already have been captured. Old content resurfaces — the Facebook photos from a decade ago, the tweets you forgot, the things you posted at university before you had a profession to protect. Tagged photographs put you in other people’s posts, outside your control, and your friends’ privacy settings are not yours.

That is a reason for care, not panic. The sensible response is a periodic digital tidy-up. Search your own name and see what an employer would see. Tighten privacy on personal accounts so photos, posts and tags are visible to friends, not the world. Review old content and remove anything you would not want a future manager, client or committee to read — not to deceive, but because youthful nonsense does not represent the professional you now are. Untag yourself from anything unhelpful. And going forward, apply the oldest test before you post: would I be comfortable if this appeared on the front of the local paper, next to my name and my employer’s?

What to leave. Everything professional, thoughtful, or simply human and harmless. You are not trying to erase yourself or become a faceless corporate cipher — a real person with genuine interests is more appealing than a blank one. Clean up the risks; keep the personality.

Part 8 — Interview preparation

If your reputation and application get you to interview, preparation is what converts it. The planners who interview well are almost never the most naturally gifted talkers; they are the ones who prepared properly, and it shows. Research the employer thoroughly — schemes, sectors, recent news, values. Research the interviewers on LinkedIn. Anticipate the questions, which for planners are often practical and case-based: talk me through a difficult application, a refusal you defended, how you deal with committee or an angry objector, a policy point relevant to the role, and why you want this job. Prepare your own thoughtful questions about the role, team and work — asking only about pay and leave does the opposite.

The STAR technique. For competency questions, answer with STAR: briefly set the Situation, the Task, the Action you took (the heart of it), and the Result. “We had a contentious 60-home application with strong objection (situation); I had to secure a recommendation members could support (task); I reworked the scheme with the applicant, ran a members’ briefing and addressed the highways concern head-on (action); it was approved with cross-party support and not appealed (result).” Far more persuasive than “I have good negotiation skills.”

Online interviews are their own small skill: a plain, tidy background; the camera at eye level (raise the laptop) so you are not looming or looking down; light on your face from in front, not a bright window behind you; a decent microphone and a tested connection; and the camera looked into, not your own image. Dress as you would in person — smart, and err on the formal side.

Interview checklistDetail
Research the employerSchemes, sectors, news, values
Research the interviewersLinkedIn backgrounds; common ground
Prepare STAR examplesOne strong story per key competency
Prepare your questionsAbout role, team and work — not just pay
Portfolio readyIf your specialism suits one
Test the tech (online)Camera at eye level, front lighting, mic, connection
Background & dressTidy plain background; smart, slightly formal
Arrive / log on earlyFive minutes, composed, not flustered

Part 9 — Building a career

Getting hired is one thing; getting on is another. After thirty years watching careers unfold, promotions rarely go to whoever served longest. They go to the people who made themselves visible, valuable and trusted — who deliver, who are known for something, and whom decision-makers can picture in the bigger role. Time served is the weakest of career strategies; reputation is the strongest.

Recruiters and employers spot future leaders by a recognisable set of signals, and every one is within your control. Deliver commercially and technically. Keep CPD and chartership current — they are the licence to progress. Develop a specialism and become genuinely expert in something scarce and valuable; the planner known as “the person for energy consents” or “the one who wins the difficult appeals” has a reputation that markets itself. Build visibility by writing the occasional article, speaking at an event, sitting on a committee. Network genuinely, so your name is in the room when opportunities arise. And — the most underrated of all — help junior staff: the planners who mentor and lift others are remembered warmly, recommended widely, and trusted with leadership.

StageWhere to put your effort
Graduate / AssistantLearn the craft; pass the APC; say yes to breadth; build good habits and a clean LinkedIn.
Planner (newly MRTPI)Deepen competence; start a specialism; take visible ownership of cases; begin contributing publicly.
Senior PlannerOwn complex work; build a reputation in your niche; mentor juniors; grow your network.
Principal PlannerLead on major/contentious work; develop commercial awareness; speak and write; manage others.
Associate / ManagerWin and lead work; build the team; be known externally; think like the business, not just the case.
Director / HeadSet direction; grow people and pipeline; be a name clients and recruiters seek out.

Part 10 — Salary progression

Reputation does not only get you hired and promoted; it shows up directly in your pay. It is one reason two planners with identical experience on paper can earn markedly different salaries. The higher earner is usually not more technically able; they are more visible, more commercially valuable, better networked and better at negotiating. A planner whose name a recruiter already knows, who is known for winning work or winning appeals, and who has options, holds cards the equally-experienced but invisible planner does not. Reputation is, quite literally, leverage.

The headlines: changing employer is usually the fastest route to a significant rise, because an external offer is benchmarked to the market while an internal one is benchmarked to your current salary. Negotiate from evidence and from your whole package, not from need, and do not be afraid to ask — in a candidate’s market, employers expect it. Treat counter-offers with caution: they fix the money but rarely the reasons you looked, and many who accept them have moved on within the year. Use a good recruiter, who knows the market rate and negotiates at arm’s length. And mind the timing: the moment of maximum leverage is when you have an offer in hand and they have decided they want you. The full mechanics are in PlanRef’s Salaries & Progression guide.

Part 11 — Fifty mistakes that damage careers

Ranked roughly from the most serious — the things that end candidacies and careers — down to the smaller habits that quietly hold people back. Few people make many of these; most make one or two without realising, which is exactly why they are worth reading in full.

Most serious — these end candidacies and careers

  1. Lying on your CV — inventing qualifications, dates or experience. Found out, it ends the process and the reputation.
  2. Misrepresenting RTPI or professional status — claiming chartership you do not hold. Checkable, and career-ending.
  3. Discriminatory, racist or hateful content online — the single fastest way to be rejected and stay rejected.
  4. A professional disciplinary finding you concealed — the concealment is often worse than the finding.
  5. Aggressive or abusive public behaviour online — pile-ons, threats, sustained hostility. Employers see a liability.
  6. Breaching confidentiality — posting about live cases, clients or employers. Instantly disqualifying.
  7. Badmouthing former employers — in interviews or online. It says more about you than them.
  8. Getting caught in a lie during interview — a single exposed exaggeration collapses all your other claims.
  9. Extreme political content, conspiracy theories or misinformation — reads as poor judgement, the one thing a professional cannot lack.
  10. Offensive “humour” — the joke you thought was harmless, screenshotted and shared.

Serious — these regularly cost jobs and promotions

  1. No LinkedIn presence at all — in 2026, an absence that reads as hiding or disengaged.
  2. A LinkedIn profile that contradicts your CV — mismatched dates or titles look like dishonesty.
  3. Public alcohol, drug or party imagery — the classic, and still one of the commonest, screen-outs.
  4. Sending the identical generic CV and cover letter to every role — recruiters can tell in seconds.
  5. Leaving the wrong company name in a cover letter — the copy-paste tell that ends applications.
  6. A CV that lists duties, not achievements — indistinguishable from every other applicant.
  7. Typos and sloppy formatting — in a detail profession, they read as carelessness.
  8. Turning up to interview having done no research — obvious, and unforgivable.
  9. Letting your chartership or CPD lapse — it closes the door to senior roles.
  10. Accepting a counter-offer for the money alone — and finding the reasons you left are still there.
  11. Burning bridges when you resign — planning is a small world with a long memory.
  12. Being unreachable or slow to reply to a recruiter or employer — reads as disinterest.
  13. Overstating seniority or fabricating an impressive-sounding job title — checkable and cheap.
  14. A careless or unprofessional email address or voicemail — small, but noticed.
  15. Being rude to junior staff, receptionists or recruiters — always gets back.

Moderate — these quietly hold you back

  1. Never negotiating salary — leaving money on the table year after year.
  2. Staying invisible — never writing, speaking or contributing to the profession.
  3. Never developing a specialism — remaining a generalist when specialists are scarce and paid more.
  4. Waiting to be promoted rather than making the case — assuming time served is enough.
  5. A weak or absent LinkedIn headline — wasting your prime keyword space.
  6. Not tailoring your profile or CV to the direction you actually want to go.
  7. Neglecting your network until the moment you need it — then it is too late.
  8. Refusing to help colleagues — being known as clever but not generous.
  9. No recommendations on LinkedIn — an easy, persuasive asset left unused.
  10. Ignoring commercial awareness — staying purely technical when leadership needs business sense.
  11. Poor interview storytelling — good work, badly told, because you never used STAR.
  12. A bad online-interview setup — dark, looming, glitchy — undercutting a strong candidate.
  13. Only ever asking about pay and leave at interview — signalling the wrong priorities.
  14. Never asking for feedback after a rejection — losing the one useful thing it offers.
  15. Job-hopping with no narrative — frequent moves with no story of progression.

Minor — the habits worth fixing

  1. An outdated CV you scramble to update only when you need it — always half a step behind.
  2. A blank LinkedIn banner and a poor photo — small signals of not bothering.
  3. Connecting with the blank default note — a missed chance to start a relationship.
  4. Over-formatting the CV with graphics an ATS cannot read.
  5. Listing every task you have ever done rather than what mattered.
  6. Vague adjectives — “passionate”, “dynamic”, “hardworking” — with nothing behind them.
  7. Never updating your profile after a promotion or a big project.
  8. Chasing LinkedIn virality instead of quiet credibility.
  9. Forgetting to proofread the very email you send the CV with.
  10. Underselling yourself out of modesty — the quiet, common mistake of good planners who never quite say what they achieved.

Part 12 — The six-month career checklist

Careers are built in small, regular steps, not occasional panics. Twice a year — put it in the calendar — spend an hour working through this. It is the single most useful habit in this guide.

  • ☐  CV: updated with recent achievements (not duties), still two pages, still ATS-clean, still honest.
  • ☐  LinkedIn: headline, about and experience current; photo and banner good; a recent recommendation requested.
  • ☐  CPD: logged and on track; a genuine knowledge gap identified and a plan to fill it.
  • ☐  Membership: subscription paid, chartership progressing or maintained, record clean.
  • ☐  Networking: three genuine connections made or renewed; one event or conversation in the diary.
  • ☐  Social media: name searched; personal accounts locked down; nothing public you would not stand behind.
  • ☐  Interview skills: two fresh STAR examples from recent work ready to tell.
  • ☐  Achievements: written down as they happen (a running list), so nothing is forgotten when it counts.
  • ☐  Career goals: reviewed — where do you want to be in two years, and is this six months moving you there?
  • ☐  Training: one skill or specialism deliberately advanced.
  • ☐  Mentoring: helping someone junior — and, whatever your stage, being mentored yourself.
  • ☐  Visibility: one thing done to be seen — a comment, an article, a talk, a committee — however small.

The personal brand audit — once a year

Ask yourselfAn honest answer means…
What am I known for?If you can’t answer in a sentence, neither can a recruiter — build a specialism.
What does a search of my name return?It should reassure, not surprise. If it doesn’t, tidy it.
Would a manager who worked with me recommend me — and for what?If you’re unsure, that’s the work: be the colleague people vouch for.
Is my public self consistent with my professional self?Contradiction breeds doubt. Align them.
If the right job came up tomorrow, am I ready to be found?CV, profile and reputation should always be quietly in order — not assembled in a panic.

That is the whole of it, really. Do the work, tell the truth, be findable, be generous, and keep the reputation that precedes you one you would be proud to walk in behind. Do that steadily, over years, and you will spend far less of your career chasing jobs — because the good ones will come looking for you.

Sources
  • StandOut CV — UK recruitment & social-media recruiting statistics (2024–25): 91% of recruiters use LinkedIn regularly; ~84% screen candidates on social media; 21% have rejected a candidate over their profile.
  • Glassdoor / aggregated employer surveys — ~91% of employers use social media in hiring; employee-reported, treated as indicative.
  • LinkedIn — official talent/help-centre guidance and 2026 industry algorithm analyses (profile, keywords, Open to Work; posting best practice — consistency, comments weighted above likes, dwell time, documents/video). The algorithm is not published; specifics are informed guidance.
  • CIPD — recruitment, selection and candidate-experience guidance, including cautions on social-media screening (fairness and bias).
  • National Careers Service, ACAS and major UK recruiters (Hays, Reed, Michael Page, Robert Walters, Penguin Recruitment, Carrington West) — CV, cover-letter, interview and negotiation good practice.
  • RTPI — chartership (MRTPI), CPD and career-progression guidance. ICO — data protection in recruitment.

Compiled 2026 for PlanRef. Statistics are attributed to their source; guidance on fast-moving platforms reflects current best practice and should be re-checked as algorithms and features change. The craft advice is drawn from long recruitment experience and offered as informed judgement.

Careers · premium · flagship guide

Winning Planning Interviews

From graduate to director — how planning interviews really work, and how to win them. United Kingdom · 2026.

Here is something that will save you a great deal of anxiety. In more than thirty years recruiting planners, from graduates to directors, the interview rarely decides who gets the job. By the time you sit down, the employer has usually already formed a view — from your application, your CV, your LinkedIn, your recommendations, the recruiter’s feedback, and often a reputation that preceded you. The interview is mostly there to confirm or contradict an impression that already exists. Candidates who understand that relax into the right task: not performing a role, but confirming, calmly and specifically, that the person on paper is real.

The other thing worth knowing is that planning interviews are not generic. Most interview advice is written by people who have never sat on a planning panel, and it shows. A local-authority interview for a development-management officer is a different animal from a housebuilder’s land-team interview, which is different again from a director-level appointment at a consultancy. The questions, the panel, the unspoken tests and the definition of a good answer all shift — and, crucially, they shift as you rise.

Inside the full guide. Twelve chapters from three decades on the hiring side — how planning recruitment really works and who decides, doing your homework, how the interview changes as you rise, a real question bank by level with what each question is testing, technical questions, STAR done well, presentations and virtual interviews, the questions you should ask, what employers are really deciding, negotiating the offer, the ranked interview mistakes, a four-week preparation plan, and the ten habits of highly employable planners — with eight tables and worked examples.

Part 1 — How planning recruitment really works

Understanding who is in the room, and who decides, changes how you prepare. Recruitment is rarely one person’s call. In a local authority, a panel typically includes the hiring manager (a team leader or DM manager), often a more senior planner or head of service, sometimes an HR representative and, for senior posts, an elected member. HR usually runs the process and guards fairness; they rarely decide, but they can veto. The hiring manager’s view carries the most weight, because they have to work with you. In the private sector it is more commercial and often less formal: an associate or director who needs a fee-earner, sometimes a two-stage process — a technical conversation then a cultural one — with the decision resting with whoever owns the team’s profit and loss.

Two things follow. First, “fit” matters more than candidates like to admit — not as a euphemism for sameness, but the panel honestly asking, “do we want to work with this person, and can we trust them?” Second, technical staff have real influence: the senior planner who quizzes you on policy may not be the decision-maker, but a doubtful word from them afterwards can sink you, and an enthusiastic one can carry you. Treat everyone in the process — including the recruiter and the person who books the room — as someone whose opinion counts. It usually does.

Part 2 — Do your homework

Preparation is where interviews are won, and it is what average candidates most neglect. Research four things — the employer, the panel, the job and the place — and you will walk in knowing more about the role than half the panel.

The employer: go well beyond the website — recent schemes and appeal decisions (the Inspectorate names the players, so you can read their actual casework), clients and sectors, standing in the trade press, company news and growth plans. The panel: research every interviewer on LinkedIn — background, tenure, RTPI involvement, anything they have written — then use it lightly to build rapport; never parade it or stray into their personal life. The job, decoded: take the job description apart sentence by sentence, separate essential from desirable, read the hidden requirements (“manage a varied caseload” means resilience and organisation; “work with members and the public” means political awareness and calm), and prepare one concrete example for every requirement.

Before a local-authority interviewBefore a private-sector interview
The adopted and emerging Local PlanThe firm’s client base and sectors
Five-year housing land supply positionThe local and regional development market
Recent and significant appeal decisionsTheir competitors and how they differ
Major allocations and growth areasLive opportunities and the development pipeline
Green Belt, conservation and constraintsHousing, commercial and employment-land demand
Neighbourhood plans and political controlStrategic land interests in the area
Current planning controversiesWhere the fees and growth are coming from

One resource candidates badly underuse is the recruiter. A good specialist has often placed people with this employer, sat through their feedback, and knows the panel, the format and what sank the last candidate. Ask them directly what the employer is really looking for — their fee depends on your success, so treat them as a coach on your side, not a gate to get past.

Part 3 — The interview changes as you rise

This is the heart of the guide. The single biggest mistake experienced planners make is interviewing the way they did ten years ago — proving they are technically competent, when the panel stopped doubting that long ago and is asking something else entirely. A director candidate who spends the interview proving they understand the NPPF has misread the room. As you rise, the weight shifts from technical mastery to leadership, commercial value and trust:

What the panel weighs, by level — illustrative and directional, not a measurement.
Technical Leadership & commercial
Graduate
Planner
Senior
Principal
Associate
Director
StageWhat the interview is really testing
Graduate / AssistantPotential, attitude, coachability — not knowledge you cannot yet have. Are you keen, honest, sensible, someone we can grow?
Planner (newly MRTPI)Applied competence — can you run cases well, largely unaided, and are you developing judgement and a specialism?
Senior PlannerReliability under pressure and ownership — can we hand you the complex, contentious work and trust the outcome?
Principal PlannerJudgement and leadership in the making — sound calls, carrying others, the commercial and political picture?
Associate / ManagerCommercial value and people — can you win and lead work, grow a team, think like the business?
Director / HeadStrategy, profit and trust — will you set direction, bring in work, manage risk and represent us?

I have watched capable senior planners talk themselves out of director jobs by answering like the brilliant senior planner they used to be — all technical mastery, no commercial or people dimension — while a less dazzling rival who talked about winning work, growing staff and carrying risk walked away with the offer. The panel was not choosing the better planner; it was choosing the person who understood which job they were interviewing for. Work out which conversation you are in, and have that one.

Part 4 — The planning question bank

A working bank of real planning interview questions by level, with what each is really testing — because the words on the surface and the thing being assessed are rarely the same. Prepare a genuine example for each that applies to you.

Graduate & Assistant

QuestionReally testing
Why planning, and why us?Genuine motivation and whether you have researched them
Talk me through a planning issue in the news that interests you.Curiosity and that you actually follow the profession
A member of the public is angry at the counter. What do you do?Temperament, common sense, and calm
Where do you see yourself in five years?Ambition, realism, and whether you will stay

Planner & Senior Planner

QuestionReally testing
Talk me through a difficult application you handled.Real ownership, judgement and how you tell a story
Describe an appeal you were involved in. What did you learn?Reflection, resilience and technical grasp
A member goes against your recommendation. How do you handle it?Professionalism, political awareness, not taking it personally
A client wants to submit against your advice. What do you do?Integrity, client management, commercial realism

Principal, Associate & Director

QuestionReally testing
How do you win work / bring in fees?Commercial value — the question that matters most here
Tell me about a scheme you rescued when it was going wrong.Judgement, leadership and composure under pressure
How do you develop and retain your team?People leadership, not just personal delivery
Tell me about a serious professional mistake and what you did.Integrity, accountability and self-awareness

Illustrative — representative of real UK planning interviews across levels, 2025–26.

Part 5 — Technical questions

Technical questions test knowledge, but more than that they test whether you can apply it — and whether you will bluff when you should not. The gravest error is pretending to know something you do not; a confident wrong answer worries a panel far more than an honest “I haven’t dealt with that directly, but my understanding is… and I’d check X”. Never bluff.

TopicA question you might getA strong answer shows
Decision-makingHow does s.38(6) shape a decision?Plan-led system understood, not just recited
Housing supplyExplain five-year land supply and why it matters.Grasp of the tilted balance and its practical effect
Green BeltHow do you approach very special circumstances?Structured judgement, not a slogan
s106 / CILWhen would you use s106 over CIL?Practical mechanism knowledge and negotiation sense
Biodiversity net gainHow has mandatory BNG changed your applications?Currency and real, recent experience
EIA / HabitatsWhen is an appropriate assessment triggered?Awareness of the legal bar, not vague familiarity
EnforcementHow do you approach expediency?Understanding it is discretionary and public-interest led
Permitted developmentWhere do Class Q / Class MA trip people up?Detail and interaction with other regimes

Depth expected rises with seniority; at director level these are rarely the focus. Confirm the current legal position before you rely on any technical detail.

Part 6 — Answering well: STAR, and standing out

For any “tell me about a time” question, answer with STAR: set the Situation briefly, the Task you faced, the Action you took — the heart of it, and always what you did, not “we” — and the Result, with a number where you honestly can. Use it for competency and scenario questions; not for simple factual or opinion questions, where a crisp direct answer is better. The trick to not sounding rehearsed is to know your stories, not your scripts — have six or seven real examples ready and tell them naturally, adapting to the question, rather than memorising word-for-word answers that collapse the moment the question is phrased differently.

What good sounds like

Q: “A member goes against your recommendation.”

Average: “I’d be professional about it and accept the committee’s decision.” True, but empty.

Excellent: “It happened on a housing scheme in a sensitive village. I’d given a clear, evidenced recommendation; members refused on character grounds. I made sure the reasons were sound and defensible, supported the decision publicly as the committee’s to make, and briefed the applicant honestly on appeal prospects. We defended it at appeal and it was dismissed — the members’ judgement held. I don’t take these personally; committees exist to weigh things differently, and my job is advice good enough to stand up either way.”

Specific, calm, politically literate — it shows judgement rather than claiming it.

You demonstrate commercial awareness by talking about value, fees, risk and delivery; professional judgement by showing how you weigh things; integrity by owning a mistake honestly; calmness by how you discuss a crisis; and leadership by talking about the people you developed. Anyone can say “I have excellent judgement”; only a good candidate can prove it with a case where their judgement was tested and held.

Part 7 — Presentations and virtual interviews

Presentations. Many senior and some mid-level interviews now include a presentation — typically ten to fifteen minutes on a set brief (“how would you grow this team”, “your approach to a live site”, “a market you would target”). It tests how you think, structure and communicate under a little pressure — exactly what the job demands in front of clients and committees. Structure it simply: a clear opening that states your answer, three or four substantiated points, and a short close that returns to the brief. Keep slides few and clean — six to ten, minimal text, one idea per slide. Rehearse to time; overrunning reads as poor judgement. Say the thing, prove it, stop.

Virtual interviews on Teams, Zoom or Meet are routine, and a poor setup undercuts a strong candidate: a plain, tidy background; the camera at eye level; light on your face from the front, not a window behind you; a decent microphone and a tested connection; your eyes to the camera, not your own image. Dress as for in person. A few discreet notes off-camera are fine — one quiet advantage of remote — but do not read from them. If the technology fails, stay calm, suggest switching to phone, and carry on; how you handle a glitch is itself a small test of composure.

Part 8 — The questions you should ask

When they ask “do you have any questions for us?”, the wrong answer is “no” — it reads as disinterest and wastes your best chance to leave a strong final impression. Questions that work well:

  • What does success look like in this role in the first year? Shows you think in outcomes, and tells you how you’ll be judged.
  • What are the biggest challenges facing the team right now? Signals realism and invites an honest, revealing answer.
  • How is the team structured, and how does work come in? Commercial curiosity — especially strong in the private sector.
  • What does progression look like from here? Ambition, and thinking beyond the job itself.
  • How would you describe the culture here — honestly? Shows fit matters to you too, and often gets a candid reply.
  • For a local authority: what are the service’s main priorities and pressures? Shows you understand the real context.
  • Is there anything about my experience you’d like me to expand on? Confident — a last chance to close any doubt.

Avoid making your only questions about pay, leave and hours (there is a time for those, and it is not now), and never ask something a moment on their website would have answered.

Part 9 — What employers are really deciding

Beneath every planning interview, whatever the questions, the panel is deciding a small number of very human things: confidence against arrogance (quiet certainty impresses; bluster alarms), your professional judgement, your commercial thinking, your self-awareness and resilience, your integrity, and your emotional intelligence. Technical ability gets you shortlisted; these qualities get you hired. The most useful way to understand it is that the panel is silently asking a series of trust questions:

  • Would we trust you with a difficult client — alone?
  • Would we let you go to committee and speak for us?
  • Would we be comfortable leaving you in front of an important developer, or a room of angry residents?
  • Would we hand you the contentious case, and sleep at night?
  • Would we want to promote you — and would others follow you?

Everything you say either builds or erodes the answer. A candidate who grasps this stops trying to impress and starts trying to reassure — which, paradoxically, is far more impressive.

Part 10 — Negotiating the offer

Negotiate after the offer, when your leverage is highest and they have decided they want you — not before. Know your whole package and the market rate (PlanRef’s Salaries & Progression guide covers this in detail), and be ready to discuss more than base pay: bonus, car allowance, pension, annual leave, hybrid working, professional subscriptions, training and CPD, promotion prospects, probation and notice. Ask professionally and from evidence, not need; in a candidate’s market a reasonable request rarely offends. Treat counter-offers from your current employer with real caution — they solve the money but seldom the reasons you looked, and many who accept them have moved on within the year. A good recruiter will negotiate for you at arm’s length.

Part 11 — Interview mistakes, ranked

Roughly from the most serious — the ones that end a candidacy on the spot — down to the smaller habits that quietly cost people offers. Most candidates make only one or two, usually without realising.

Serious — these end interviews

  1. Bluffing a technical answer — a confident wrong answer worries a panel more than an honest “I don’t know”.
  2. Lying or exaggerating — and being caught, which collapses everything else you said.
  3. Badmouthing a current or former employer — it says more about you than them.
  4. Breaching confidentiality — discussing live cases or clients in detail. Instantly disqualifying.
  5. Arrogance — dismissing others, talking over the panel, treating the interview as beneath you.
  6. Criticising a scheme the interviewer worked on — do your homework so you never blunder into it.
  7. Having done no research — obvious within minutes, and unforgivable.
  8. Being unable to talk through a single real case — for an experienced planner, fatal.
  9. Blaming others for every setback — no accountability, no trust.
  10. Rudeness to junior staff or the recruiter — it always gets back to the panel.

Costly — these lose offers

  1. Rambling, unstructured answers that never reach a point.
  2. Vague, “we”-heavy answers that hide what you actually did.
  3. Not knowing the authority’s housing land supply or Local Plan position.
  4. At senior level, still trying to prove technical competence instead of leadership.
  5. Reciting policy instead of applying it.
  6. Memorised, robotic answers that collapse when the question is rephrased.
  7. No questions for the panel, or only questions about pay and leave.
  8. Talking too much and never letting the panel steer.
  9. Talking too little — one-line answers that give them nothing to hire.
  10. Overrunning a presentation, or reading the slides aloud.
  11. Poor virtual setup — dark, looming, glitchy, distracted.
  12. Failing to give concrete examples — all claim, no evidence.
  13. Negotiating hard before an offer is even on the table.
  14. Showing no genuine interest in this employer specifically.
  15. Being unable to explain your reasons for leaving convincingly.

Avoidable — these leave a dent

  1. Arriving flustered or logging on late.
  2. Dressing too casually and misreading the employer’s formality.
  3. Fidgeting, poor eye contact, low energy.
  4. Not preparing questions and asking something the website answered.
  5. Failing to link your answers back to their role and their needs.
  6. Undue modesty — never quite saying what you achieved.
  7. Waffling when you don’t know, instead of a clean, honest answer.
  8. Forgetting the names or roles of the people interviewing you.
  9. No follow-up — missing the short, courteous thank-you that costs nothing.
  10. Treating a “junior” interviewer as unimportant.

Part 12 — The four-week preparation plan

Preparation spread over a month beats a panic the night before. Compress proportionately for shorter notice.

WhenDo this
Weeks 3–4 beforeResearch the employer, their schemes, appeals and market in depth. Decode the job description and list every requirement. Research each interviewer on LinkedIn; read up on the area.
Week 2 beforePrepare six or seven real STAR stories covering the key competencies. Refresh the technical areas the role demands. Draft your questions; if a presentation is required, build and start rehearsing it.
Week 1 beforeMock interview with a colleague or mentor — out loud, to time. Rehearse the presentation. Read the week’s planning news. Test the technology and sort your setup, background and lighting.
Day beforeLight review of your stories — do not cram. Confirm logistics: time, place, format, names. Prepare what to wear. Sleep.
On the dayArrive or log on early and composed. Re-read the job description once. Then trust the preparation and be yourself.

The ten habits of highly employable planners

Interviews are won in the years before them, not the hour of them. These are the long-term habits that separate the planners who are always in demand from the ones who struggle.

  1. They keep learning. CPD is a habit, not a box; a stale planner is soon a stuck one.
  2. They become known for something. A specialism — “the one for energy consents”, “the one who wins the hard appeals”.
  3. They tell the truth. To clients, members, colleagues and interviewers. Integrity is the whole foundation.
  4. They deliver. Reliably, and they finish what they start. Nothing builds a reputation faster, or quieter.
  5. They think commercially. Even in the public sector — value, risk and delivery, not only policy.
  6. They stay calm. Under pressure, in hostile rooms, when it goes wrong. Composure is remembered.
  7. They help others. They mentor, share and lift juniors — and are recommended warmly for it for years.
  8. They keep their reputation in order. CV current, LinkedIn strong, footprint clean — ready to be found.
  9. They build relationships before they need them. A real network, tended in the good times.
  10. They own their mistakes. Admit, learn, move on — which, far from damaging trust, is what earns it.

Do these, steadily, and the interview looks after itself. You will walk in as someone the panel already half-wants to hire, spend an hour confirming what your reputation promised, and spend far less of your career chasing jobs — because the good ones will be coming to find you.

Compiled 2026 for PlanRef. Draws on long UK planning-recruitment experience; recruitment statistics cited elsewhere in the Careers section are attributed to their sources (StandOut CV, CIPD, National Careers Service). Interview practice evolves — treat the craft as informed guidance, and the technical detail as a prompt to confirm the current position before you rely on it.

Careers

Careers

Everything for your planning career in one place — choose one below. Live planning jobs, a career-guidance assessment, a salary and progression explorer, and a recruiter’s guide to the reputation that gets you hired.

Planning jobs · free

Planning Jobs

Planning jobs from across the profession — filtered to genuine town-planning roles, not the financial-planner and production-planner noise. Browse and search here and open each job in the app; you apply on the employer or source site.

Loading jobs…

Free · wellbeing & coping

Wellbeing & Coping in Planning

Planning is a caring profession that is not always good at caring for the people who do it. This section is about the pressure the job carries, what genuinely helps, and what senior colleagues and organisations can do — written for planners and other public-facing professionals. Honest, evidenced, and practical. England / UK.

If you are struggling, help is available now. Samaritans — free, day or night, 116 123. Shout — 24/7 crisis text line, text 85258. In immediate danger, call 999.
Free · Wellbeing & Coping · anchor article

Under Pressure

Mental health, the public-facing job, and what senior colleagues can do. Wellbeing for planners and other public-facing professionals — the scale of the problem, the public-facing injury, what Covid and home working changed, the value of senior colleagues, a programme of support, the duties behind it, and where to turn. England / UK; written as at 21 July 2026.

Free · Wellbeing & Coping

Coping Under Pressure

Maintaining perspective in a demanding profession — healthy habits and a steady head for a long career. Telling the pressure you can ride from the stress that grinds, keeping a sense of proportion, handling criticism and confrontation, leaving work at work, the unglamorous basics that actually work, attention and breathing, a life outside planning, and what to do when you are no longer coping. England / UK.

Free · Wellbeing & Coping

Staying in Control

Managing workload in a demanding profession — practical habits for getting the work done without letting it get on top of you. Why the job got heavier, the interrupted day, deciding what matters first, the inbox, “good enough”, delegation, protected thinking time, and a pace that lasts a career. England / UK.

Free · Wellbeing & Coping

You’re Not Alone

The value of mentoring and professional support, and why successful planners rarely succeed on their own. Nobody masters it all; what a mentor really is; the learning that is never in a textbook; tending your network; looking out for each other; and why asking for help is a mark of competence, not weakness. England / UK.

Premium · Climate & Environment · anchor article

Planning for a Changing Climate

Beyond the megaproject — the proven toolkit, the honest limits, and technology’s real role. How planning actually cuts carbon and copes with a hotter, wetter climate: density and walkability, trees and cooling, flood and heat resilience, retrofit and embodied carbon, and why the built-from-scratch eco-city keeps failing. Mitigation and adaptation kept apart, every claim tagged for maturity and confidence. England / UK; written as at 21 July 2026.

Premium · Climate & Environment · article 2

The Bus Stop and the Bike Store

Human-scale climate measures in everyday planning — a practical, checklist-led guide. The companion to “Planning for a Changing Climate”: where that article made the big argument, this one comes down to the scheme in front of you. England; written as at 21 July 2026.

Climate change is a vast problem, and planning’s response is often discussed at a vast scale — net zero, strategic energy, the decarbonisation of whole cities. All of that matters, but it is not where most planners spend their days. Most spend them on ordinary applications: a barn conversion, a small housing site, an extension, a change of use, a fifty-home scheme on the edge of a town. And it is precisely there, on the everyday application, that a development-management planner holds a set of small levers the strategic debate tends to overlook.

A bike store by the front door rather than behind the bins. A bus stop you can see from the houses. A roof left clear for solar. A front garden kept as garden rather than paved for a car. None saves the planet on its own — but they are cumulative, they are within an ordinary planner’s gift, and, placed well, they quietly shape how people live for the sixty-year life of the building. Get the bike store and the bus stop right and you nudge a thousand daily journeys out of the car, every day for decades. That is a small thing that adds up.

A checklist companion. The heart of this is a set of tick-box checklists — one per theme — to run at pre-application, on validation and before committee, and to scale to the development: a full run for a major scheme, a light touch for a householder. Factual points about the Building Regulations and the law carry a confidence tag; where the piece takes a view it is marked as a PlanRef view.
Inside the full article. Ten short chapters and six ready-to-use checklists — movement, energy & fabric, water, waste & circular economy, greening & cooling, and climate-proofing — plus what planning can and cannot require after the Future Homes Standard, how to secure measures through policy, conditions and s106, and an honest take on carbon and offsetting.

Two disciplines run through all of the below. First, proportionality — scale the ask to the development; demanding a car-club bay on a single dwelling brings the whole exercise into disrepute. Second, honesty about what planning can actually require, which is where we start.

2. What planning can, and cannot, require

A great deal of a building’s climate performance is now set by the Building Regulations, not planning, and a planner who does not know the boundary will either ask for things they cannot secure or duplicate what the Regulations already deliver. Fabric efficiency, the heating system, ventilation, EV charge points and overheating are matters for the Regulations. EV charging in new homes with parking has been required under Part S since June 2022, and overheating mitigation under Part O since the same date.

The bigger shift is the Future Homes Standard. The Government published its response and the amended Building Regulations in March 2026; the standard comes into force on 24 March 2027 (with a transition to 2028), and it is a step change. New homes must emit at least 75% less carbon than a 2013-standard home, cannot use fossil-fuel heating (a heat pump or heat-network connection, not a gas boiler), and must include on-site renewable generation — in practice solar over much of the roof. The consequence for planners: from 2027 the new home is low-carbon by default, so your attention shifts to what the Regulations do not cover — how people move, how the place is shaped, greening, water, waste storage, and the layout that makes the low-carbon choice the easy one.

One significant exception to “leave energy to the Regulations” is worth knowing, because it is often mis-stated. A December 2023 Written Ministerial Statement discouraged local plans from setting energy standards above the Regulations. But in July 2025 the Court of Appeal confirmed the Statement does not have the force of law and does not override authorities’ statutory power, under the Planning and Energy Act 2008, to set higher local energy standards where justified by local evidence (R (Rights: Community: Action) v SoS [2025] EWCA Civ 990). So a sound, evidenced local policy can still ask for more than the national baseline. For the everyday DM planner, though, the rule holds: don’t gold-plate the Regulations case by case, and put your energy into the human-scale measures planning genuinely owns.

3. Getting there without a car

This is the single most valuable area a planner controls, because how a development connects to the world around it locks in travel behaviour for the life of the scheme, and transport is one of the hardest parts of the economy to decarbonise. The prize is not banning cars; it is making the walk, the cycle and the bus the easy, obvious choice — and small design decisions decide whether they are. On major applications, Active Travel England is now a statutory consultee, which gives active-travel provision real weight.

Movement checklist — run at pre-application and on layout
  • ☐  Walking. Direct, safe, overlooked routes to where people actually go — shops, school, stop, centre. Desire lines followed, not fought.
  • ☐  Crossings. Safe, convenient crossings where routes meet roads; no severance that pushes people back into the car.
  • ☐  Cycle parking. Secure, covered, accessible storage by the front door — not behind the bins — to LTN 1/20 standard, with room for cargo bikes.
  • ☐  The bus. A stop within an easy walk, with shelter, seat and real-time information; bus priority or a service contribution where the scheme can support one.
  • ☐  Connections. Genuine links into the existing walking and cycling network — permeable for people, not a cul-de-sac island.
  • ☐  EV and car clubs. EV charging (Part S) provided; a car-club bay on larger schemes to cut the need to own a car at all.
  • ☐  Layout. Front doors onto streets, active frontages, low speeds by design — because design, not signage, changes behaviour.

4. The building and its energy

From 2027 the Future Homes Standard does much of this for you, so the planner’s role is smaller than people assume — but not nothing, and it matters most on schemes the Standard does not reach (conversions, changes of use, and everything built before it bites). Focus on what planning shapes: orientation and form that work with sun and daylight; roofs kept clear and structurally ready for solar; space and routes for a heat pump; and connection to a heat network where one exists. And, above all, the measure with the largest carbon saving — keeping and reusing the existing building rather than demolishing it, because the greenest building is the one that already exists.

Energy and fabric checklist
  • ☐  Reuse first. Has genuine retention and reuse of the existing building and its materials been explored before demolition is accepted?
  • ☐  Orientation. Does the layout let homes benefit from daylight and passive solar, without designing in overheating (Part O)?
  • ☐  Solar-ready roofs. Are roofs oriented, unshaded and structurally able to take PV — installed now, or ready for it?
  • ☐  Heat pumps. Is there sensible, well-designed space for air-source heat pumps and their external units, considering noise and appearance?
  • ☐  Heat networks. Where a district network exists or is planned, has connection been secured or safeguarded?
  • ☐  No gold-plating. Are energy asks either within an evidenced local policy or left to the Regulations — not invented case by case?

5. Water

Water is the quiet climate issue — parts of England are seriously water-stressed, and a hotter climate makes both drought and flash flooding worse. New homes are built to a baseline water-efficiency standard, but where a local plan is justified an authority can require the tighter optional standard of 110 litres per person per day by condition. Sustainable drainage does double duty — managing flood risk and, through planting, cooling and biodiversity.

Water checklist
  • ☐  Efficiency. Where justified locally, the 110 l/p/d optional standard secured by condition.
  • ☐  Sustainable drainage. SuDS designed in from the start — swales, basins, rain gardens — that hold and slow water rather than pipe it away.
  • ☐  Permeable surfaces. Permeable materials for drives, paths and parking; resist sealing gardens and forecourts under impermeable hardstanding.
  • ☐  Rainwater. Water butts as standard; rainwater harvesting on larger or non-residential schemes.
  • ☐  Planting. Drought-resilient, low-water planting that survives hotter, drier summers without irrigation.

6. Waste and the circular economy

Waste storage is the most neglected corner of a housing layout and one of the easiest to get right — and it has a real behavioural effect: if recycling and food-waste bins are hard to reach, badly sited or too small, people do not use them. Designing storage in properly — accessible, adequate, and no further from the door than the general-waste bin — quietly lifts recycling rates for the life of the scheme. With food-waste collections rolling out across England, adequate, well-placed storage is no longer optional.

Waste and circular-economy checklist
  • ☐  Designed-in storage. Adequate, accessible storage for general, recycling and food waste — convenient, or it will not be used.
  • ☐  Food and compost. Food-waste storage provided; space for home composting on schemes with gardens.
  • ☐  Reuse of buildings. Existing buildings retained and reused where possible, saving their embodied carbon.
  • ☐  Reuse of materials. Reclaimed and low-embodied-carbon materials encouraged; demolition arisings reused on site where practical.
  • ☐  Construction waste. A site waste and demolition approach that designs out waste rather than skipping it.

7. Greening and cooling

Green infrastructure is the best-evidenced adaptation measure there is, and much of it is delivered at exactly the human scale this article is about — a tree in the right place, a hedge instead of a fence, a green roof, a front garden kept green. Trees shade and cool streets that will otherwise bake; planting slows water; and since 2024 most development must deliver a 10% biodiversity net gain (major development from February 2024, small sites from April 2024), which planning secures and, crucially, must see maintained. The commonest failure is not the requirement but the follow-through — the tree that dies unwatered, the net gain unmanaged after year two.

Greening and cooling checklist
  • ☐  Trees for shade. New and retained trees where they shade streets, homes and hard surfaces — secured with the space, soil and, above all, maintenance to survive.
  • ☐  Protect what exists. Existing mature trees and hedgerows retained; a semi-mature tree cannot be replaced by a sapling for thirty years.
  • ☐  Biodiversity net gain. The 10% delivered well, genuinely additional, and — the hard part — secured and managed long-term, not just drawn on a plan.
  • ☐  Green roofs and walls. Green or brown roofs where they earn their place, for cooling, water and biodiversity.
  • ☐  Gardens over paving. Real gardens and permeable planting resisted from being paved over for parking; front gardens kept green.
  • ☐  Greening the streets. Verges, street trees, rain gardens and hedgerows built in, not value-engineered out.

8. Designing for a changing climate

Adaptation is the half of climate action planning has quietly always done, and at the human scale it is about making sure the homes and places built today will still be comfortable and safe in the hotter, wetter climate they will live through. Much of it costs little if designed in early, and a great deal if retrofitted later.

Climate-proofing checklist
  • ☐  Overheating. Orientation, shading, ventilation and glazing that keep homes cool in summer (Part O for new homes; a live issue for conversions and the existing stock).
  • ☐  Flood resilience. Development steered away from flood risk, and where it must go there, made safe — raised levels, resilient materials, safe access.
  • ☐  Shade and shelter. Trees, canopies and built shade in public spaces, streets and play areas for a hotter climate.
  • ☐  Future-ready. Homes and layouts that can adapt — provision for future solar, EV charging, heat pumps and cooling without tearing the place up.

9. Carbon, counted honestly

A word on carbon and offsetting, because it is where good intentions most often slide into greenwash. The discipline is a hierarchy, and the order matters: reduce first, then supply low-carbon, and only then offset the small remainder. Reducing the carbon a scheme emits and embodies — through reuse, fabric, low-carbon materials and the measures above — always comes before buying it away. Embodied carbon deserves real attention on larger schemes, and whole-life carbon assessment is becoming normal.

10. How to actually secure it

None of this matters unless it ends up in the permission, and the mechanisms are the ones planners use every day. The earlier you raise these measures the cheaper they are to secure, so pre-application is where most of the value is won — before the design is fixed and viability set. A sound, evidenced local plan policy is the strongest foundation. Conditions secure many measures (the water standard, cycle parking, landscaping, EV beyond the Regulations), provided each meets the six tests — necessary, relevant to planning, relevant to the development, enforceable, precise and reasonable. Larger or off-site measures — a bus contribution, off-site tree planting, a genuine carbon-offset payment, long-term BNG management — run through section 106.
