And design review, the National Model Design Code and local codes give the greening and movement measures real weight.

Two cautions close the loop. Keep it proportionate: the full checklist is for a major scheme; a householder application needs a light touch, and asking a single extension for a travel plan is the kind of overreach that discredits the whole agenda. And keep it viable and lawful: every measure lands in the same viability appraisal as affordable housing and infrastructure, and a condition that fails the six tests, or an energy ask with no policy behind it, will not survive an appeal. Used with judgement, these small levers are the part of climate action a planner can pull on every single application — and, added across a career and a district, they amount to a great deal more than they look.

PlanRef view — opinion

Reduce first. Don’t gold-plate. Offset last. An offset contribution has its place — funding real, local, additional and monitored reduction, such as tree planting or the retrofit of existing homes. But it is a last resort for the genuine remainder, not a line a developer buys to avoid designing properly, and a “carbon-neutral” badge resting on cheap, unverifiable offsets is worth exactly nothing. Apply the same scepticism you bring to biodiversity and nutrient credits: additional, secured, monitored, local — or discounted. And on the everyday scheme, a well-placed bus stop, a decent bike store, a shade tree and a reused building save real carbon at almost no cost, while the grand gesture with the green label often saves none. The small, honest measure beats the large, marketed one — a view, but after two decades of evidence, a confident one.

Practice points

  • Small, cumulative, and yours. The everyday measures — bike store, bus stop, shade tree, reused building — are the part of climate action a DM planner touches on every application. They add up.
  • Know the Building Regs boundary. Energy, EV (Part S), overheating (Part O) and, from 24 March 2027, the Future Homes Standard (75% less carbon, no gas, solar) are Regs matters. Don’t duplicate or gold-plate them case by case.
  • But local energy policy survives. The Court of Appeal (July 2025) confirmed LPAs keep the power to set higher local energy standards where justified. Evidenced policy, not case-by-case invention.
  • Movement is the biggest lever. Make the walk, cycle and bus the easy choice through layout, safe routes, a real bus stop and a bike store by the door. It locks in travel behaviour for sixty years.
  • Water counts. The 110 l/p/d optional standard by condition where justified; SuDS and permeable surfaces that manage flood and heat together.
  • Design waste storage in. Accessible, adequate recycling and food-waste storage lifts recycling for the life of the scheme; hard-to-reach bins get ignored.
  • Green it — and maintain it. Shade trees, hedgerows and a well-delivered 10% BNG. The failure is follow-through: secure the maintenance, not just the plan.
  • Reduce first, offset last. Cut and reuse before you buy carbon away. Treat offsets like credits — additional, secured, monitored, local, or worth nothing.
  • Secure it early and proportionately. Pre-app is where the value is won; use policy, six-tests conditions and s106; scale the ask to the development, and keep it viable and lawful.
Sources
  • Future Homes and Buildings Standards; the Building Regulations etc. (Amendment) (England) Regulations 2026 (Government response March 2026; in force 24 March 2027) — the 75%-less-carbon standard, the end of fossil-fuel heating, and mandatory solar for new homes.
  • Building Regulations Approved Document S (EV charging, 2022) and Approved Document O (overheating, 2022) — what the Regulations, not planning, now require in new homes.
  • R (Rights: Community: Action) v SoS [2025] EWCA Civ 990 (Court of Appeal, July 2025); Planning and Energy Act 2008; Written Ministerial Statement, December 2023 — that LPAs retain the power to set higher local energy standards where justified.
  • Optional technical housing standard for water efficiency (110 l/p/d) — the tighter standard an authority can require by condition where its local plan justifies it.
  • Biodiversity net gain — mandatory 10% (major development February 2024; small sites April 2024).
  • Active Travel England (statutory consultee for larger applications, 2023); Manual for Streets; LTN 1/20 Cycle Infrastructure Design; National Model Design Code; Simpler Recycling.

Written as at 21 July 2026 (England). Factual points on the Building Regulations, standards and law are attributed to their sources; the PlanRef view box is clearly-marked opinion. Standards and thresholds change — re-check before relying on any point on a live application, and keep the ask proportionate, viable and lawful.

Premium library

Specialist Guides

Practitioner guides by Steve Hesmondhalgh MRTPI, converted for mobile reference. Premium in full. Further guides follow.

Specialist guide

Annexes, Outbuildings & Curtilage

The law at each topic's surface; the judgment beneath it.

Specialist guide

Enforcement: A Practical Guide

Enforcement is discretionary, personal, and won or lost on drafting and evidence. Six topics from the practitioner's guide.

Specialist guide

Caravans & Mobile Homes

Two overlapping regimes, deceptively simple statutory language, and outcomes that turn on facts. Until you know whether the structure is a caravan in law, nothing else can sensibly follow.

Specialist guide

Lawful Development Certificates

Lawfulness is a state of affairs, not a document. The certificate confirms it; evidence decides it; time rules it. Six topics from the practitioner's guide.

Specialist guide

Advertisement Control

A parallel consent regime with its own logic: three routes worked in sequence, and every judgment confined to two grounds. Six topics from the practitioner's guide.

Specialist guide

Rural Workers' Dwellings

Few applications in rural planning are harder to win. The exception turns on one word — essential — and the case is built in order: functional need, scale, finance, alternatives, then the dwelling and its conditions. Six topics from the practitioner's guide.

Specialist guide

Class MA: Commercial to Residential

The principal route from shops, offices and clinics to housing without a planning application. The permission comes from the Order; the authority's say is confined to a closed list of matters — and the schemes that fail, fail on light, noise and the gateway tests. Six topics from the practitioner's guide.

Specialist guide

Class Q: Agricultural Buildings to Dwellings

Wholly substituted from 21 May 2024: two gateways, ten dwellings, 1,000 square metres, and eligibility that turns on history, fact and structure. The most technically demanding right in Part 3 — and the one that rewards forensic preparation most. Seven topics from the practitioner's commentary.

Specialist guide · policy

Settlement Boundaries

The oldest tool in the rural policy kit is about to carry presumption-switching weight — and most lines on today's proposals maps were never drawn under any stated methodology. From a comparative review of twenty-one published evidence documents: the common core, the design choices, and the method that survives examination. Written against the December 2025 draft NPPF; review to follow the final Framework. Six topics.

Specialist guide

Householder Permitted Development

GPDO Part 1 applied day to day. The rights read simple and measure hard — this guide sets out the allowances as they stand today and where the complications lie. The government technical guidance was last revised in 2019 and does not cover additional storeys at all.

Primary sources — always check the live text

GPDO 2015, Schedule 2, Part 1 — consolidated text as it stands today ↗
MHCLG technical guidance for householders (2019 edition) ↗

General reference, not advice on a specific property. Part 1 cases turn on measurements taken on site — verify against the live text before anything is built.
Specialist guide

Varying Permissions: s73, s73A & Phased Schemes

Almost nothing large is built exactly as first permitted. This guide covers the three routes to changing a permission after grant — section 73 and its Finney/Fiske limits, section 73A for development already built, and the Pilkington/Hillside line that governs phased and masterplan schemes, where the wrong move can sterilise the whole permission. England; current to July 2026.

Specialist guide

Nutrient Neutrality & the Habitats Regulations

The water-quality bar on development. How a habitats duty becomes a hard stop on housing and overnight accommodation; the case law that hardened it; the science of phosphate and nitrate; what triggers a requirement and what does not; how a nutrient budget is built and read critically; the mitigation routes and their catches; a consultant’s playbook; the boundary with Wales and with ordinary drainage conditions; and where the reforms are heading. England; current to July 2026.

Specialist guide

How Planning Applications Are Handled

The procedural rulebook — the Development Management Procedure Order 2015. What makes an application valid, how it must be publicised and consulted, the time periods and extensions, the decision notice and its reasons, non-determination and appeals, the public register, and how the courts read procedural failures. Procedure, not policy. England; current to July 2026.

Specialist guide

Heritage: Significance & Harm

The concept the whole subject turns on. What significance is and how to establish it; the statutory duties that overlay every decision; setting beyond the view; the categories of harm; why “less than substantial” is not “less than substantial weight”; the two balances and how not to double-count; and archaeology. It then sets out the six-step significance-and-harm method, three worked assessments, and what appeals consistently show. England; current to July 2026.

Specialist guide

Decision-Making & Material Considerations

The spine of development management: the structure of judgement every other guide leans on. Section 38(6) and the two-stage method; materiality versus weight; the hierarchy of the plan, the Framework and the minister; the tilted balance; the duties that constrain rather than weigh; the reasons that decide; and the red herrings that are not material at all. The framework is then worked through eight common application types, from a householder extension to a major greenfield scheme. England; current to July 2026.

Specialist guide

Planning Conditions & Discharge

Every permission arrives wearing conditions, and most of the trouble in practice starts there. The law of imposing them, the machinery of discharging and varying them, and the case law that decides — sometimes decades later — whether a permission was ever implemented at all. England; current to July 2026.

Free reference · England · beta

Appeal Finder

Search the Planning Inspectorate appeal record by authority, site, appeal type (Planning, Householder, Enforcement, LDC, Advertisement and so on) and outcome. Results open here in the app; the full decision letter is one tap away on the Inspectorate portal.

Scope. Structured search of the full Planning Inspectorate casework record (England) — around 185,000 appeal and related decisions across the decade to June 2026 — find appeals by authority, type, outcome, date and reference. Cases from 2021 onward carry the full site address and a live portal link; older records are indexed by postcode and authority (the legacy dataset omits the street address) and their legacy references may not resolve on the current portal. This is a finding aid; each decision turns on its own facts. Contains public sector information licensed under the Open Government Licence v3.0.
Free reference · England

Case Law & Appeals

The court judgments and the key appeal decisions behind the PlanRef guides, in one place. Search by name, citation or subject, filter to court or appeal, and open the guide topic where each is discussed.

Scope. A finding aid to the authorities behind the guides — the leading court judgments and a curated, growing set of Planning Inspectorate appeal decisions — not a comprehensive digest. Appeal decisions turn on their own facts and bind no one else; always read the decision or judgment in full, and check it remains good law, before relying on it. Appeal decisions contain public sector information licensed under the Open Government Licence v3.0.
Free reference · England

Land Use Gazetteer

What class is it? Start typing a use — the gazetteer suggests matches and gives the classification an authority would ordinarily be expected to reach.

Read this before relying on it. No statute lists every use: these are the classifications ordinarily expected in England, but the Use Classes Order names very few uses directly, authorities differ where it is silent, and the primary use of the planning unit — not the sign over the door — decides. Conditions on a permission can restrict changes the Order would otherwise allow, and Wales and Scotland run different systems. Verify against the Order as it stands today ↗ and, where the answer matters, with the local planning authority.
Free reference · A–Z

Planning Dictionary

The most commonly used planning and built environment terms, explained in plain English. Based on current practice in England — a companion to legislation and policy, not a substitute for them.

Quick reference

Practitioner Tools

Who decides? — National Scheme of Delegation

From 31 October 2026, whether an application is decided by officers or by committee is fixed nationally by SI 2026/854 — local schemes of delegation are superseded for the listed functions. Choose the application type and scale to see the route, whether it can reach committee, and how. England.

Legal basis: s.54 Planning and Infrastructure Act 2025 (ss.319ZZC–319ZZF TCPA 1990) · TCP (Discharge of Local Planning Authority Functions) (England) Regulations 2026, SI 2026/854 · in force 31 October 2026 · MHCLG statutory guidance (1 June 2026). A finding aid, not legal advice — consult the SI and the guidance.

Judicial Review & Statutory Challenge — route & deadline

How to challenge a planning decision in the High Court — judicial review or the statutory route — with the correct deadline. Different decisions take different routes, with different, mostly non-extendable time limits, and the wrong route or a missed date is usually fatal. Tap what you want to challenge to see the route, the court, whether the court’s permission is needed, and the deadline, with a date calculator. England.

A finding aid for planners — spot the route, clock the deadline, know when to instruct counsel. Not legal advice; these deadlines are mostly non-extendable, so confirm the exact date and take advice before relying on it. England · checked 23 July 2026.

Ecological survey calendar

When protected-species surveys can be done — and how the seasons bite on your programme and start date. Search by species, by month, or by English region. Indicative windows from Natural England’s standing advice (England); weather-, location-, altitude- and method-dependent.

PremiumThe full calendar — every protected species, four-state windows, and the programme and start-date implications — is a Premium tool. Search by species, month or nation.
Indicative windows only · confirm scope and timing with a suitably qualified ecologist and the relevant statutory nature-conservation body · Checked Jul 2026

Nutrient neutrality — catchment finder & budget

Find whether a location falls in a nutrient-neutrality catchment, the protected site and nutrient of concern, and the official Natural England calculator — then run an indicative residential budget. England; 27 catchments.

PremiumCatchment finder and the indicative nutrient-budget calculator are a Premium tool.
Catchment boundaries are hydrological — confirm the site on Natural England’s map. The official catchment calculator is the tool of record · Checked Jul 2026

Heritage & archaeology site check

Define a site by postcode, place, grid reference or dropped pin, and screen designated heritage within a chosen radius — listed buildings, scheduled monuments, conservation areas, registered parks & gardens, battlefields and World Heritage Sites — ranked by distance with links to each List entry, plus signposts to the local Historic Environment Record, Heritage Gateway, PAS finds and LIDAR terrain, and a dated export for the project file. A first-look screening aid, not an HER search or heritage assessment: the absence of a designation does not mean there is no archaeology.

Designated data: planning.data.gov.uk (Historic England & LPA), OGL v3.0 · LIDAR © Environment Agency · Checked Jul 2026

Appeal deadline calculator

Calendar-accurate deadlines. Always verify against the current Planning Inspectorate guidance before relying on a date.

Site visit checklist

The constraint datasets to interrogate for any site, each linked to its authoritative source. Add a site reference to label the checklist; your ticks are saved on this device. For live constraint mapping by location, use the Site Intelligence overlay.

Use classes at a glance

Each class links to the live Use Classes Order on legislation.gov.uk, and to Part 3 of the GPDO for permitted changes between classes — always showing the legislation as it stands today.

Change of use — is permission needed?

The usual position for a change between use classes in England. A signpost to the right route, not a determination — the class limits, conditions and any article 4 direction still govern.

GPDO 2015 Sch 2 Pt 3 · UCO 1987 · Checked Jul 2026

LPA finder

Every local planning authority in England. Type an authority or district name to confirm it and open its planning pages.

Enforcement time limits

Answer two questions for the applicable immunity period and the earliest immunity date. England only; transitional rules applied automatically.

s171B TCPA 1990 as amended · LURA 2023 s115 · SI 2024/452 reg 5 (transitional) · Checked Jul 2026

Authority status — presumption indicators

Where an authority sits against the Housing Delivery Test, and the paragraph 79 consequence that follows. An indicators tool, not a determination — the presumption can also be engaged by a shortfall in five-year housing land supply or by out-of-date policies, which this cannot know.

Forecast the current position

PremiumPremium unlocks the paragraph 79 consequence, the plan-age indicator, the interactive forecast, and the link into the tilted-balance guidance.

Appeal analytics — LPA success rates

Allowed rates from the Planning Inspectorate casework record. These describe past outcomes only — every appeal turns on its own facts, and a rate is not a prediction. Rates cover substantively decided appeals (allowed or dismissed); withdrawn, invalid and split decisions are reported separately, never inside the rate. Any figure resting on fewer than 10 decisions is suppressed.

PremiumPremium unlocks per-authority allowed rates, the appeal-type and procedure breakdowns, the year-by-year trend and the national comparison.

Application fees

Fees are set nationally and subject to periodic indexation. Always compute from the current fee regulations or the Planning Portal calculator rather than remembered figures — remembered figures are how underpaid fees stall validation.

Fee Regulations (as amended) · Planning Portal fee calculator
Guide

Lawful development certificates

A s192 certificate asks "if I do this, will it be lawful?"; a s191 certificate asks "is what I did now lawful because the enforcement period has passed?". The test is lawfulness, not merit — no policy, no balance of harm, onus entirely on the applicant on the balance of probabilities. Certificates deal in facts: contemporaneous documents beat retrospective statements every time.

PremiumThe full six-topic guide — immunity, evidence, concealment and appeals — is a Premium feature. Free members see the opening of each topic.
s191–s192 TCPA 1990

Community Infrastructure Levy

CIL is a fixed, non-negotiable charge some authorities levy on new floorspace, set by a charging schedule that has been examined and approved — it applies only where an authority has one in force. It sits alongside, not instead of, s106 obligations. Rates are charged per square metre, indexed annually, and the reliefs that matter — self-build, residential extensions and annexes, charitable and social housing — must be claimed and confirmed before commencement. Starting work first is the classic and irreversible mistake. For a fuller working explanation see Planning Geek\u2019s CIL guide ↗.

Source: MHCLG list of charging schedules (page updated 10 June 2026) · PlanRef check Jul 2026 · double-check on GOV.UK ↗

PremiumPremium members can search every charging authority\u2019s CIL status — approved, under examination or withdrawn, with in-force dates — kept updated against the MHCLG record.
Planning Act 2008 Pt 11 · CIL Regulations 2010 (as amended) · Checked Jul 2026

Legislation finder

The instruments behind English planning practice — each opening the live, consolidated text on legislation.gov.uk, with quick links to the key Parts, Schedules and sections. Type to filter.

Every link opens the version as in force today. Summaries are a navigation aid, not a substitute for the instrument itself \u2014 always read the provision that applies.

PlanRef provides guidance and signposting, not planning advice. Professional judgment remains with the user. England only.

Subscribing opens secure checkout with Payhip. Once subscribed, your access code is waiting in the PlanRef members area — copy it, return here, and choose Enter access code. Your rate is locked for as long as you subscribe.

Free — always, no account needed
  • First Principles — the full foundations essay
  • Appeal Finder — search 185,000 real Inspectorate decisions
  • The planning dictionary, use classes and the everyday tools
  • Any authority’s Housing Delivery Test score, and the national appeal-success figures
  • The opening of every specialist guide

  • Practitioner Notes on every guide topic

Welcome to PlanRef

Built around your practice

Three details shape the guides and tools around your work. Stored on this device only — nothing leaves your browser.

What are your main areas of interest?
